title: Choosing The Right AI Tool
summary: Learn how to match tasks to tool types, evaluate tradeoffs, and build a small stack that fits your real work.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Tool Types, Not Tool Hype
lessons.subtitle: Think in capabilities first: chat, search, workspace, embedded AI, and automation.
lessons.objective: Classify AI tools by job-to-be-done so you can choose based on workflow fit instead of brand familiarity.
lessons.useWhen: Think in capabilities first: chat, search, workspace, embedded AI, and automation.
lessons.whyItMatters: Teams lose time when they switch tools for novelty instead of capability. A capability-first view reduces tool sprawl.

coreConcepts.title: General Assistants
coreConcepts.text: Best for drafting, summarizing, outlining, and structured thinking when you can provide the needed context.

coreConcepts.title: Research-Oriented Tools
coreConcepts.text: Best when recent information, link tracing, and source visibility matter more than broad drafting flexibility.

coreConcepts.title: Embedded AI
coreConcepts.text: Best when the strongest advantage is access to work already living in email, docs, chat, or your existing stack.

workedExample.scenario: A business analyst uses one chat tool for everything, including fresh market research and internal document drafting.
workedExample.approach: They separate the work into recent-information tasks, internal-document tasks, and writing tasks.
workedExample.result: They stop forcing one tool to cover every mode of work.
workedExample.verification: They compare output quality and review effort across three real tasks before deciding which tools stay in their stack.

doThisNow.task: List the last five AI tasks you attempted and label which capability type each one actually needed.
steps: Label the tool type that fits it best.
Circle where you used the wrong mode.

commonMistakes: Using a familiar tool for every task.
Comparing tools without testing the same workflow.
Mistaking marketing claims for workflow fit.

roleVariants.manager: Frame AI requests around a deliverable, risk level, and reviewer before you ask for output.
roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

sourceNotes: Reviewed for business IC relevance and durable workflow guidance.
Keep verification and organizational policy checks explicit in future revisions.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Selecting Between General Assistants
lessons.subtitle: Choose based on workflow fit, source handling, and the kind of help you need.
lessons.objective: Create a practical rubric for choosing among general AI assistants without relying on stale feature lists.
lessons.useWhen: Choose based on workflow fit, source handling, and the kind of help you need.
lessons.whyItMatters: Tool capabilities change frequently. A decision rubric survives updates better than memorizing comparisons.

coreConcepts.title: Start With Your Work
coreConcepts.text: Evaluate tools against writing, synthesis, document review, and analysis tasks you already do every week.

coreConcepts.title: Judge The Review Burden
coreConcepts.text: A tool that gives a decent draft with low cleanup can be more valuable than one that looks smarter in a demo.

coreConcepts.title: Consider Trust And Access
coreConcepts.text: Workspace integration, source visibility, and company approval may matter more than raw model quality.

workedExample.scenario: A manager debates between two assistants based on online opinions.
workedExample.approach: They test both on a meeting summary, policy rewrite, and planning memo using the same inputs and rubric.
workedExample.result: The choice becomes obvious because one tool fits their actual review burden and integration needs better.
workedExample.verification: They compare draft quality, time-to-final, and whether the tool exposes enough evidence for checking.

doThisNow.task: Build a four-column scorecard for any two AI assistants you are considering.
steps: Run the same input through both tools.
Score which one created less cleanup.

roleVariants.writer: Use AI as a drafting and editing partner, then review for voice, facts, and audience fit.
roleVariants.analyst: Treat AI as a hypothesis generator and explainer, not as a trusted source of record.
roleVariants.operator: Anchor every prompt in the real workflow, inputs, and downstream handoff.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: When Research Tools Win
lessons.subtitle: Recent information and source traceability require a different evaluation standard.
lessons.objective: Know when to switch from a drafting assistant to a research-oriented tool or a manual search workflow.
lessons.useWhen: Recent information and source traceability require a different evaluation standard.
lessons.whyItMatters: The wrong tool can make fresh-information tasks look easier than they are, while quietly introducing unsupported claims.

coreConcepts.title: Freshness Matters
coreConcepts.text: If the answer depends on current information, look for tools or workflows that expose sources and timestamps.

coreConcepts.title: Traceability Matters More
coreConcepts.text: A weaker answer with clear links is often better than a polished answer with no visible evidence.

coreConcepts.title: Research Still Needs Review
coreConcepts.text: AI can speed triage and synthesis, but you still decide what evidence is credible enough to use.

workedExample.scenario: A writer needs current policy language for a client note.
workedExample.approach: They use a source-forward research workflow, then bring verified excerpts back into a drafting assistant.
workedExample.result: The final note is both current and well-written.
workedExample.verification: They open the linked sources and confirm the exact language before publishing.

artifact.label: Decision Rule
artifact.title: Switch to research mode when
items: The answer depends on recent events.
You must defend the evidence to another person.

doThisNow.task: Identify one task this week where you should use a source-first workflow instead of a general chat prompt.
steps: Decide how you will verify the sources.

commonMistakes: Asking a general assistant for live facts without checking.
Sharing research summaries with no links.
Confusing a confident answer with a sourced answer.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Build A Small, Stable Stack
lessons.subtitle: Most people need fewer tools, not more.
lessons.objective: Decide what belongs in your personal stack and what should stay on a watch list.
lessons.useWhen: Most people need fewer tools, not more.
lessons.whyItMatters: Too many tools create cognitive load, inconsistent habits, and duplicated subscriptions with little added value.

coreConcepts.title: Keep A Core Stack
coreConcepts.text: One general assistant, one research workflow, and the AI already embedded in your primary workspace is enough for most business ICs.

coreConcepts.title: Use A Watch List
coreConcepts.text: Capture interesting tools without adopting them immediately. Curiosity is good; constant switching is expensive.

coreConcepts.title: Adopt For A Task
coreConcepts.text: Add a tool only when it clearly improves a recurring task you already care about.

workedExample.scenario: An operator has five subscriptions but still uses only two each week.
workedExample.approach: They map each tool to a recurring workflow and cancel the ones with no clear role.
workedExample.result: Their stack gets smaller and their habits improve.
workedExample.verification: They measure whether the remaining tools cover the same work with less switching.

artifact.label: Inventory
artifact.title: Keep / watch / cancel
items: Keep what improves a recurring task.
Watch what looks promising but unproven.
Cancel what adds no clear workflow value.

doThisNow.task: Sort your current AI tools into keep, watch, and cancel.
steps: Write the task each tool supports.
Mark how often you actually use it.
Cancel or pause one tool that lacks a clear role.

commonMistakes: Keeping tools for hypothetical future use.
Adding tools before mastering the current stack.

lessons.sequenceRationale: This lesson exists here to move the learner from understanding the pattern to applying it on a live work artifact.
lessons.title: Evaluate New Tools Without Distraction
lessons.subtitle: Use a simple test plan before changing your workflow.
lessons.objective: Create a lightweight evaluation habit for new AI tools and features.
lessons.useWhen: Use a simple test plan before changing your workflow.
lessons.whyItMatters: The field changes quickly. A repeatable evaluation approach lets you stay current without becoming reactive.

coreConcepts.title: Test On Real Work
coreConcepts.text: The only fair evaluation is a task you already do, with the same review standard you would use in production.

coreConcepts.title: Measure Adoption Cost
coreConcepts.text: Setup time, switching friction, and training needs count just as much as output quality.

coreConcepts.title: Delay Optional Decisions
coreConcepts.text: Interesting does not mean urgent. Most tools can wait until they prove a real workflow advantage.

workedExample.scenario: A coordinator is tempted by a new AI note-taking app.
workedExample.approach: They test it on one live meeting, compare the output to their current process, and score the cleanup burden.
workedExample.result: They make a grounded decision in one short session instead of weeks of low-grade distraction.
workedExample.verification: They compare accuracy, follow-up usefulness, and the effort needed to onboard the rest of the team.

artifact.label: Test Plan
artifact.title: 30-minute tool evaluation
items: Score quality, review effort, and switching cost.

doThisNow.task: Write a 30-minute evaluation plan for the next AI tool you are curious about.
steps: Name the task you will test.
List what would make the tool not worth adopting.

nextModuleBridge: Next, use the right tool choices to write sharper prompts and reusable workflows.
